story.title: Dr. Williams: Calculating the Rocket's Push
story.text:
My name is Dr. Jerome Williams. I am an aerospace engineer at SpaceX. I use Newton's Laws to plan our rocket missions. These laws explain how forces make things move.

Our rockets have a lot of mass. Mass is how much stuff is in an object. This mass gives the rocket inertia. Inertia is the resistance to changing motion. To launch, our engines create a huge applied force. This push must be greater than the forces pushing back, like air friction.

The total force that moves the rocket is the net force. A large net force causes fast acceleration. This means the rocket's speed increases quickly. If we increase the rocket's mass with more cargo, we need more force to get the same acceleration.

This is just like pushing a shopping cart. An empty cart is easy to push. A full cart has more mass and more inertia. You need a bigger push to make it accelerate. My calculations ensure our rockets have the right push for their mass.
think_abouts: Why does a rocket need such powerful engines to lift off?
How is a full shopping cart similar to a heavy rocket when you try to push it?
story.go_deeper: Dr. Williams mentions air friction. In space, there is almost no air. How does this lack of friction help a spacecraft travel long distances to other planets without using its engines the whole time?

margin_defs.definition: how much stuff is in an object

check_prompts: What happens to acceleration if you increase the net force on an object?

vocabulary.definition: The total force on an object when you add all individual forces together, considering their directions.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The net force was strong enough to make the box slide across the floor.

vocabulary.definition: The rate of change of an object's speed or direction over time.
vocabulary.use_sentence: When the driver hit the gas pedal, the car's acceleration pushed us back in our seats.

vocabulary.definition: A force that slows down moving objects when they are in contact with a surface.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The friction between the brake pads and the wheel stopped the bike.

vocabulary.definition: The property of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. It is related to mass.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Because of inertia, you feel your body move forward when a car suddenly stops.

discussion_cards.prompt: Think about kicking a soccer ball and a bowling ball with the same force. Which one will have greater acceleration and why?
discussion_cards.frame: If I kick both balls with the same force, the ___ will accelerate more because it has less ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: Describe a time you felt inertia. What was happening to make you feel it?
discussion_cards.frame: I felt inertia when I was in a ___ and it suddenly ___. My body wanted to keep ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: How does friction help you in your daily life? How can it also make things more difficult?
discussion_cards.frame: Friction helps me when I ___, but it makes things harder when I try to ___.

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
